rationale_for_baseline_tier: An advanced obstructing oesophageal lesion has decompensated into an acute emergency: the patient cannot swallow even saliva/water, is actively aspirating (coughing/choking on liquids, hoarse, with a developing chest infection), cannot maintain hydration, and is frail, cachectic and bedbound with signs of hypovolaemia. These are patient-reportable, time-critical features (inability to take any intake with aspiration, unable to protect the airway from saliva, too weak to self-transport). This warrants an immediate 000 emergency ambulance to hospital with strict nil-by-mouth — the ceiling above T3 is justified because the patient can themselves report a genuine emergency, not merely because a clinician wanted an exam. T5 is the honest tier; the source note dispatched an ambulance for direct admission.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Gasping for air, blue lips (cyanosis), or worsening breathing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — tell 000 to upgrade the priority
escalation_edges.rationale: Aspiration or airway obstruction is an immediate threat to life and mandates the highest-priority ambulance response.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he starts gasping for air or his lips turn blue, call 000 straight back and tell them his breathing is failing so they upgrade the emergency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting a large amount of bright red blood (haematemesis)
escalation_edges.rationale: A large upper-GI bleed from the tumour is a haemorrhagic emergency requiring the highest-priority response and airway protection.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he vomits a large amount of bright red blood, ring 000 again immediately and tell them — sit him leaning forward so blood does not go into his lungs.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Choking he cannot clear, or collapse / loss of consciousness before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.rationale: Airway soiling with collapse can precede respiratory arrest; positioning and immediate paramedic resuscitation are the critical resource.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he chokes and cannot clear it, or collapses, roll him onto his side, keep his airway clear, and tell 000 immediately — start CPR if he stops breathing and you know how.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult (~68), cachectic, bedbound and profoundly weak from weeks of failing nutrition
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reduced physiological reserve — dehydration, aspiration and infection are poorly tolerated and can deteriorate fast; do not delay emergency transport, and do not attempt any oral intake to 'keep him going'.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult child present at the bedside and able to help
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Enlist the family member to call/stay on the line with 000, keep the patient upright and nil-by-mouth, unlock the door for paramedics, and watch for the airway/bleeding escalation triggers.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an emergency ambulance right now and say he cannot swallow, is coughing up everything he tries to drink, and is very weak. Give him NOTHING by mouth — no food and not even a sip of water — because the blockage means fluid goes into his lungs. Sit him fully upright, leaning slightly forward, and have him spit saliva into a tissue rather than swallow it. Stay with him, unlock the front door for the paramedics, and watch for gasping, blue lips or vomiting blood.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or a routine appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will arrange urgent endoscopy with biopsy and staging imaging and coordinate the oesophago-gastric cancer pathway; the GP should receive the discharge summary and support ongoing multidisciplinary and smoking-cessation care.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as a routine or urgent-but-non-emergency dysphagia/reflux problem (GP review, 2-week-wait referral) rather than an emergency.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Continued aspiration and dehydration at home leading to aspiration pneumonia, hypovolaemic shock and death while awaiting a routine appointment.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising the alarm features but routing to same-week clinic/endoscopy without emergency transport or nil-by-mouth.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is the correct tier here, so over-triage is not a meaningful risk; the dominant error direction is under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The specific diagnosis (oesophageal cancer) cannot be confirmed remotely — it requires endoscopy, biopsy and imaging.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the recognised alarm-feature and aspiration/dehydration emergency without over-claiming the diagnosis: escalate by emergency ambulance, instruct nil-by-mouth and positioning, and arrange urgent inpatient endoscopy/specialist assessment.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either withholding escalation because the diagnosis is unconfirmed, or asserting a confirmed cancer diagnosis over a video call.
